source_metadata.title_raw: William L. McClure - A-18-273 - February 14, 2019 - Menlo Park

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
William L. McClure 
City Attorney 
Office of the City Attorney 
1100 Alma Street 
Menlo Park, CA 94025 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-273 
Dear Mr. McClure: 
February 14, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act") 1 and Government Code section 1090. Please note that we are 
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice 
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts 
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.2 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Mateo County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(S).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. Does the Act prohibit _Councilmember Drew Combs, who is employed by Facebook, from 
taking part in property-related applications filed with the City by Facebook, by an owner of 
property leased to Facebook, or by property owners in close proximity to Facebook? 
2. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Combs from taking part in amendments to the 
City's General Plan or the Transportation Master Plan? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Because your questions regarding the provisions of the Act are general in nature, we are providing only 
informal assistance in response to your questions under the Act. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with 
the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 


File No. A-18-273 
Page No. 2 
3. Assuming Councilmember Combs has a financial interest in a development agreement 
between the City and Facebook, would the remote interest in Section 1091(b)(2) apply to allow the 
City to nonetheless enter into or amend an agreement with Facebook? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. The Act prohibits Councilmember Combs from taking part in property-related 
applications filed by Facebook or by an owner of property leased to Facebook. Moreover, the Act 
would prohibit him from taking part in property-related applications by owners of property in close 
proximity if a prudent person would-find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision would 
contribute to a change in Facebook's stock price. 
2. The Act prohibits Councilmember Combs from taking part in decisions such as the 
General Plan amendments or Transportation Master Plan if Facebook is explicitly involved in the 
decision. Accordingly, Councilmember Combs is prohibited from taking part in decisions initiated 
by Facebook. In addition, the Act would prohibit him from taking part in amendments to the 
General Plan or the Transportation Master Plan if the Face book is the subject of the action or the 
action is directed solely at Facebook. Accordingly, Councilmember Combs may be prohibited from 
taking part in the decisions if they are limited or directed towards Facebook or its property. For 
other decisions not initiated by Facebook and not otherwise limited or directed towards Facebook or 
its property, Councilmember Combs is prohibited from taking part in the decisions only if a prudent 
person would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision would contribute to a change in 
Facebook's stock price. 
3. The exception under Section 1091(b)(2) would apply to allow the City to enter into or 
amend an agreement with Facebook so long as Councilmember Combs discloses his financial 
interest in the agreement, that disclosure is noted in the official records, and he abstains from any 
participation in making the agreement. 
· 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
You are the City Attorney for the City of Menlo Park seeking advice on behalf of 
Councilmember Combs regarding his position as a Facebook employee.3 Councilmember Combs 
and his family are tenants in a single-family home located on Oakland Avenue in Menlo Park. 
Facebook engages in the development of social media applications for people to connect 
through mobile devices, personal computers, and other platforms. It enables users to share opinions, 
ideas, photos, videos, and other activities online. Its products include Facebook, Instagram, 
Messenger, WhatsApp, and Oculus. Facebook is headquartered in Menlo Park, California and 
employs approximately 25,110 people, of which approximately 14,000 are located in Menlo Park. 
3 We note that Councilmember Combs participated in City Council discussions concerning the proposed tenant 
relocation assistance ordinance on February 12, 2019. The Commission does not provide advice regarding past conduct. 
(Regulation 18329(b )(8)(A).) Therefore, we do not analyze the proposed ordinance with respect to his leasehold interest 
in his residence. 


Facebook Employment 
File No. A-18-273 
Page No. 3 
Councilmember Combs has worked for Facebook since May 2015. He is currently a 
manager on the scaled support team and his duties include managing support interfaces and support 
content to help Facebook's community have great experiences on Facebook. His position does not 
involve work on real estate development or transactions. Councilmember Combs is a full-time 
salaried employee who owns less than three percent of Facebook stock. In addition, Councilmember 
Combs' wife is employed as a Content Manager at WhatsApp, a Facebook owned company. 
Facebook Owned Property 
Facebook itself or through a wholly owned subsidiary owns and has developed three 
campuses in the Bayfront Area of Menlo Park. The East Campus is a 56.9-acre site that contains 
nine buildings occupied by approximately 6,600 employees. The West Campus is an approximately 
22-acre site that contains one building. The Expansion Campus is an approximately 58-acre site that 
contains three office buildings.4 For each of these three developments there are Development 
Agreements which include community benefit payments and guaranteed on-going revenue streams 
for the City of Menlo Park. 
Additionally, Facebook owns property located at 1350 Willow Road in the Bayfront Area, 
which consists of approximately 59 acres. On July 6, 2017, Peninsula Innovation Partners, LLC, on 
behalf of Facebook, submitted a formal application to comprehensively redevelop the site. The 
proposed master plan project would include approximately 1,500 residential dwelling units, 1. 72 
million square feet of office space, 126,500 square feet of retail space, a 200-room hotel and a 
40,000 square foot cultural/visitors center. The public process begins with a City Council study 
session on the proposed project (anticipated to be scheduled in the first quarter of 2019), and then 
after the project moves through the planning process, City Council review of the application 
components, including a Conditional Development Permit and Development Agreement and an 
Environmental Impact Report. The City Council must make the final decision on the entitlements. 
In total, Facebook is the largest single landowner in the Bayfront Area of Menlo Park, 
owning 34.5 percent of the property. With respect to the entire City, Facebook owns about six 
percent of the total land area. 
In addition to the property owned by Facebook, Facebook leases an additional 46.16 acres in 
the Bayfront Area, some of which may be under contract to purchase and some is under long-term 
ground leases allowing Facebook to redevelop the properties. 
General Plan 
In November 2016, the City of Menlo Park adopted a General Plan update commonly 
referred to as ConnectMenlo. ConnectMenlo was a comprehensive multi-year process that 
identified a vision for a live/work/play environment in the Bayfront Area, while maintaining the 
character and values embraced throughout the city. The land use changes in the Bayfront Area 
included adoption of three new zoning districts, rezoning of property and increasing net new 
4 The Expansion Campus has an approval for inclusion of a 200-room limited service hotel. 


File No. A-18-273 
Page No. 4 
development potential up to 2.3 million square feet of non-residential uses, up to 4,500 residential 
units and up to 400 hotel rooms. 
An Environmental Impact Report ("EIR") was prepared that analyzed ConnectMenlo's 
proposed development potential. The amount of development potential studied in the EIR and 
incorporated into ConnectMenlo was less than the maximum development potential allowed by the 
new zoning districts and is considered a development cap. Any new development potential above 
the amount identified in ConnectMenlo and studied in the EIR would require additional 
environmental review and an amendment to the General Plan, both of which would require approval 
by the City Council. 
Since the adoption of ConnectMenlo, a number of property owners and prospective 
applicants, including Facebook, have either filed formal development applications or expressed 
interest in redeveloping property within the Bayfront Area. Given that the currently proposed 
projects would exceed the maximum office gross floor area by 768,614 square feet, it is anticipated 
that the number and size of proposed projects in the Bayfront Area would exceed all of the office 
development potential within the current cap. 
In the first quarter of 2019, the City Council will consider options to address this issue. 
One option would be to maintain the current development cap. Depending on the timeline for 
individual projects, some could potentially be approved under the current cap. However, other 
projects that are larger or more complicated (such as Facebook's proposed master plan project at 
1350 Willow Road) would have longer review times and may not be able to be reviewed and 
approved under the current development cap. Those projects would need to be reduced in size to 
comply with the development cap or initiate a project specific General Plan amendment and 
conduct the necessary environmental review. Another option would be for the City Council to 
authorize the City to initiate an amendment to the development cap. With a City-initiated General 
Plan amendment, the City would incur significant additional consultant costs to conduct the 
planning process and necessary environmental review. 
Transportation Master Plan 
The City of Menlo Park is now undertaking a Transportation Master Plan process, which 
when completed, would provide a detailed vision, set goals and performance metrics for network 
performance, and outline an implementation strategy for both improvements to be implemented 
locally and for local contributions towards regional improvements. Following completion of the 
Transportation Master Plan, the Transportation Impact Fee program would be updated to modernize 
the City's fee program to collect funds towards construction of the improvements identified and 
prioritized in the Master Plan. It is anticipated that most of the transportation improvements will be 
located in the Bayfront Area. As the largest single landowner and a significant ground lessee in the 
Bayfront Area, it is anticipated that Face book will pay for the majority of these improvements that 
will be identified in the Transportation Master Plan and funded through the Transportation Impact 
Fee program. 
In addition, Face book has been part of the regional discussion regarding reactivation of the 
Dumbarton Rail Corridor and other transportation improvements related to Bayfront Expressway 
and Willow Road east of US 101. Facebook has already contributed funds for studies and it is 


File No. A-18-273 
Page No. 5 
anticipated that it will continue to participate voluntarily as well as be required to contribute 
financially as a significant amount of the transportation capacity in these areas. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, parti~ipating 
in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that would have 
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests 
distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An 
official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act 
are set forth in Section 87103 and include all of the following: 
• 
Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or 
more (subdivision (a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, 
or holds any position of management (subdivision (d)). 
• 
Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Subdivision (b ). ) 
• 
Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value to the official in the 12 months prior 
to when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (c).)5 
• 
Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $500 or more to the official in the 12 months prior to 
when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (e); Regulation 18940.2.) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances or those of immediate family members. 
Based on the facts provided, Councilmember Combs would have an interest in Facebook as 
a business entity and source of income. He would also have an interest in WhatsApp, the employer 
of his spouse, as a source of income. Assuming he does not have a periodic tenancy of one month or 
less, he would have a real property interest in his current residence. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Foreseeability standards vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a 
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regulation 18701(a) 
establishes that a financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real 
property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). 
5 "Income" includes "any community property interest in the income ofa spouse." (Section 82030.) 


File No. A-18-273 
Page No. 6 
For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial 
effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
Development Applications for Property Owned/Leased by Face book or in Close Proximity 
In situations where Facebook is the applicant, it will be explicitly involved as the named 
party of any property-related applications such as land use permits or entitlements; and the financial 
effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(a).) 
This financial effect is material under Regulations 18702.l(a)(l) and (a)(5), as Facebook is 
the applicant. Under the Act, the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial 
effect on his financial interest, and Councilmember Combs must recuse himself pursuant to 
Regulation 18707. 
However, Facebook would not be explicitly involved in any applications for property 
development submitted by property owners in close proximity to Facebook; and the financial effect 
of the decision on Councilmember Combs' interest would be reasonably foreseeable and material if 
a "prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's 
(Facebook's) publicly traded stock ... " (Regulations 18702.l(b) and 18702.3(a)(4).)6 
Note that the determination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts involved in 
each decision. Therefore, we recommend you contact our office for additional advice with respect 
to a particular decision on such applications before the City of Menlo Park. 
General Plan Amendments and Transportation Master Plan 
Similar to property-related applications, if Facebook initiates a project specific General Plan 
amendment or the Transportation Master Plan, the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable 
and material financial effect on Councilmember Combs' financial interest under Regulations 
18702.l(a)(l) and (a)(5), and he would have to recuse himself pursuant to Regulation 18707. 
Moreover, Face book is explicitly involved in a decision and the effect of the decision is 
material if Face book is the subject of the action or the action is directed solely at Face book. 
(Regulation 18702.l(a)(6) and (7).) Accordingly, Councilmember Combs may be prohibited from 
taking part in the decisions if the decisions are limited or directed towards Facebook or its property. 
However, if another entity, such as the City, initiated amendments to the General Plan or the 
Transportation Master Plan, and the decisions were not limited or directed towards Facebook or its 
property, Face book would not be explicitly involved; and the financial effect of the decision on 
Councilmember Combs' interest would be reasonably foreseeable and material only if a prudent 
person with sufficient information finds it reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the decision 
6 Regulation 18702.1 (b)(l)-(6) list factors to be considered in determining whether the decision's effect will be 
material. 


File No. A-18-273 
Page No. 7 
would contribute to a change in Facebook's stock price. (Regulations 18702.l(b) and 
18702.3(a)(4).) 
As mentioned, the determination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts 
involved in each decision. At this time, we do not have sufficient information regarding any 
proposed General Plan amendments to complete a conflict of interest analysis. In regard to the 
Transportation Master Plan, you have stated that Facebook will pay for the majority of proposed 
improvements. However, we do not currently know the allocation between Facebook and any other 
person subject to the fee or the magnitude or nature of the transportation projects proposed. 
Accordingly, we are unable to determine the extent that the decisions are limited or directed 
towards Facebook. 
However, to the extent that the decision is not limited or directed at Facebook, we 
emphasize that Councilmember Combs is disqualified from the decision only if it is foreseeable that 
the decision would contribute to a change in Facebook's stock price. While this determination 
ultimately depends on the magnitude of the specific transportation projects proposed, we note that it 
is unlikely that transportation projects would contribute to a change in Facebook's stock price 
unless the projects were of considerable magnitude. 
To the extent you need additional assistance regarding Master Plan amendments or the 
Transportation Master Plan, we recommend you contact our office for additional advice with 
respect to a particular decision before the City of Menlo Park providing a full description of the 
nature of the decision. 
Section 1090 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. 
Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) 
An. officer is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her agency's 
contracts when the officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the 
contract at issue. (Thomson, supra, at p. 649.) As a general rule, when Section 1090 is applicable to 
one member of a governing body of a public entity, the entire governing body is precluded from 
entering into the contract. (Stigall, supra, at p. 569.) 
Your facts presume that Councilmember Combs has a financial interest in any new or 
existing development agreement between the City and Facebook - you therefore inquire whether he 
has a remote interest under Section 109l(b)(2) such that the City may nonetheless amend or enter 
into development agreements with Facebook. 
Section 1091 (b )(2) establishes that a "remote interest" exists where: (1) the contracting 
party has ten or more employees other than the officer; (2) the officer was employed by the 
contracting party at least three years prior to initially joining the public body; (3) the officer owns 


File No. A-18-273 
Page No. 8 
less than three-percent of the stock in the contracting party; (4) the officer is not an officer or 
director of the contracting party; and (5) the officer did not directly participate in formulating the 
bid of the private contracting party. 
Here, Facebook employs more than ten employees. Councilmember Combs is not an officer 
of Facebook, he was a Face book employee for at least three years prior to taking a seat on the City 
Council, he owns less than three-percent of Facebook's stock, and he did not directly participate in 
any real estate matters that are relevant to any existing or future development agreements with the 
City. The same is true for his spouse who works for WhatsApp. Therefore, Councilmember Combs 
has a remote interest in the City's new or amended development agreements with Facebook under 
Section 1091(b)(2). 
When a "remote interest" is present, a board may execute a contract: (1) the officer in 
question discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest 
is noted in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the 
making of the contract. (Section 1091(a); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) Therefore, the 
City Council may enter into or amend an agreement with Facebook so long as Councilmember 
Combs discloses his financial interest in the agreement, that disclosure is noted in the official 
records, and he abstains from any participation in making the agreement. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
0 ~ 
r Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1. Does the Act prohibit _Councilmember Drew Combs, who is employed by Facebook, from 
taking part in property-related applications filed with the City by Facebook, by an owner of 
property leased to Facebook, or by property owners in close proximity to Facebook? 
2. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Combs from taking part in amendments to the 
City's General Plan or the Transportation Master Plan?2 Because your questions regarding the provisions of the Act are general in nature, we are providing only 
informal assistance in response to your questions under the Act. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with 
the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)  
3. Assuming Councilmember Combs has a financial interest in a development agreement 
between the City and Facebook, would the remote interest in Section 1091(b)(2) apply to allow the 
City to nonetheless enter into or amend an agreement with Facebook?
sections.conclusion:
1. The Act prohibits Councilmember Combs from taking part in property-related 
applications filed by Facebook or by an owner of property leased to Facebook. Moreover, the Act 
would prohibit him from taking part in property-related applications by owners of property in close 
proximity if a prudent person would-find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision would 
contribute to a change in Facebook's stock price. 
2. The Act prohibits Councilmember Combs from taking part in decisions such as the 
General Plan amendments or Transportation Master Plan if Facebook is explicitly involved in the 
decision. Accordingly, Councilmember Combs is prohibited from taking part in decisions initiated 
by Facebook. In addition, the Act would prohibit him from taking part in amendments to the 
General Plan or the Transportation Master Plan if the Face book is the subject of the action or the 
action is directed solely at Facebook. Accordingly, Councilmember Combs may be prohibited from 
taking part in the decisions if they are limited or directed towards Facebook or its property. For 
other decisions not initiated by Facebook and not otherwise limited or directed towards Facebook or 
its property, Councilmember Combs is prohibited from taking part in the decisions only if a prudent 
person would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision would contribute to a change in 
Facebook's stock price. 
3. The exception under Section 1091(b)(2) would apply to allow the City to enter into or 
amend an agreement with Facebook so long as Councilmember Combs discloses his financial 
interest in the agreement, that disclosure is noted in the official records, and he abstains from any 
participation in making the agreement. 
·

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from making, parti~ipating 
in making, or using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision that would have 
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests 
distinguishable from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An 
official's financial interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act 
are set forth in Section 87103 and include all of the following: 
• 
Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $2,000 or 
more (subdivision (a)), or in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, 
or holds any position of management (subdivision (d)). 
• 
Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Subdivision (b ). ) 
• 
Any source of income aggregating $500 or more in value to the official in the 12 months prior 
to when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (c).)5 
• 
Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating $500 or more to the official in the 12 months prior to 
when the decision at issue is made. (Subdivision (e); Regulation 18940.2.) 
• 
An interest in the official's personal finances or those of immediate family members. 
Based on the facts provided, Councilmember Combs would have an interest in Facebook as 
a business entity and source of income. He would also have an interest in WhatsApp, the employer 
of his spouse, as a source of income. Assuming he does not have a periodic tenancy of one month or 
less, he would have a real property interest in his current residence. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Foreseeability standards vary depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in a 
governmental decision. An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a decision, and is 
presumed to be reasonably foreseeable, if the interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a 
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regulation 18701(a) 
establishes that a financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision affecting a real 
property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). 
5 "Income" includes "any community property interest in the income ofa spouse." (Section 82030.)  
For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision at issue, the financial 
effect of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ). ) 
Development Applications for Property Owned/Leased by Face book or in Close Proximity 
In situations where Facebook is the applicant, it will be explicitly involved as the named 
party of any property-related applications such as land use permits or entitlements; and the financial 
effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(a).) 
This financial effect is material under Regulations 18702.l(a)(l) and (a)(5), as Facebook is 
the applicant. Under the Act, the decisions will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial 
effect on his financial interest, and Councilmember Combs must recuse himself pursuant to 
Regulation 18707. 
However, Facebook would not be explicitly involved in any applications for property 
development submitted by property owners in close proximity to Facebook; and the financial effect 
of the decision on Councilmember Combs' interest would be reasonably foreseeable and material if 
a "prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in the price of the business entity's 
(Facebook's) publicly traded stock ... " (Regulations 18702.l(b) and 18702.3(a)(4).)6 
Note that the determination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts involved in 
each decision. Therefore, we recommend you contact our office for additional advice with respect 
to a particular decision on such applications before the City of Menlo Park. 
General Plan Amendments and Transportation Master Plan 
Similar to property-related applications, if Facebook initiates a project specific General Plan 
amendment or the Transportation Master Plan, the decision would have a reasonably foreseeable 
and material financial effect on Councilmember Combs' financial interest under Regulations 
18702.l(a)(l) and (a)(5), and he would have to recuse himself pursuant to Regulation 18707. 
Moreover, Face book is explicitly involved in a decision and the effect of the decision is 
material if Face book is the subject of the action or the action is directed solely at Face book. 
(Regulation 18702.l(a)(6) and (7).) Accordingly, Councilmember Combs may be prohibited from 
taking part in the decisions if the decisions are limited or directed towards Facebook or its property. 
However, if another entity, such as the City, initiated amendments to the General Plan or the 
Transportation Master Plan, and the decisions were not limited or directed towards Facebook or its 
property, Face book would not be explicitly involved; and the financial effect of the decision on 
Councilmember Combs' interest would be reasonably foreseeable and material only if a prudent 
person with sufficient information finds it reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the decision 
6 Regulation 18702.1 (b)(l)-(6) list factors to be considered in determining whether the decision's effect will be 
material.  
would contribute to a change in Facebook's stock price. (Regulations 18702.l(b) and 
18702.3(a)(4).) 
As mentioned, the determination of a conflict of interest is dependent upon the facts 
involved in each decision. At this time, we do not have sufficient information regarding any 
proposed General Plan amendments to complete a conflict of interest analysis. In regard to the 
Transportation Master Plan, you have stated that Facebook will pay for the majority of proposed 
improvements. However, we do not currently know the allocation between Facebook and any other 
person subject to the fee or the magnitude or nature of the transportation projects proposed. 
Accordingly, we are unable to determine the extent that the decisions are limited or directed 
towards Facebook. 
However, to the extent that the decision is not limited or directed at Facebook, we 
emphasize that Councilmember Combs is disqualified from the decision only if it is foreseeable that 
the decision would contribute to a change in Facebook's stock price. While this determination 
ultimately depends on the magnitude of the specific transportation projects proposed, we note that it 
is unlikely that transportation projects would contribute to a change in Facebook's stock price 
unless the projects were of considerable magnitude. 
To the extent you need additional assistance regarding Master Plan amendments or the 
Transportation Master Plan, we recommend you contact our office for additional advice with 
respect to a particular decision before the City of Menlo Park providing a full description of the 
nature of the decision. 
Section 1090 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. 
Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) 
An. officer is conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her agency's 
contracts when the officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the 
contract at issue. (Thomson, supra, at p. 649.) As a general rule, when Section 1090 is applicable to 
one member of a governing body of a public entity, the entire governing body is precluded from 
entering into the contract. (Stigall, supra, at p. 569.) 
Your facts presume that Councilmember Combs has a financial interest in any new or 
existing development agreement between the City and Facebook - you therefore inquire whether he 
has a remote interest under Section 109l(b)(2) such that the City may nonetheless amend or enter 
into development agreements with Facebook. 
Section 1091 (b )(2) establishes that a "remote interest" exists where: (1) the contracting 
party has ten or more employees other than the officer; (2) the officer was employed by the 
contracting party at least three years prior to initially joining the public body; (3) the officer owns  
less than three-percent of the stock in the contracting party; (4) the officer is not an officer or 
director of the contracting party; and (5) the officer did not directly participate in formulating the 
bid of the private contracting party. 
Here, Facebook employs more than ten employees. Councilmember Combs is not an officer 
of Facebook, he was a Face book employee for at least three years prior to taking a seat on the City 
Council, he owns less than three-percent of Facebook's stock, and he did not directly participate in 
any real estate matters that are relevant to any existing or future development agreements with the 
City. The same is true for his spouse who works for WhatsApp. Therefore, Councilmember Combs 
has a remote interest in the City's new or amended development agreements with Facebook under 
Section 1091(b)(2). 
When a "remote interest" is present, a board may execute a contract: (1) the officer in 
question discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest 
is noted in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the 
making of the contract. (Section 1091(a); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248 (2000).) Therefore, the 
City Council may enter into or amend an agreement with Facebook so long as Councilmember 
Combs discloses his financial interest in the agreement, that disclosure is noted in the official 
records, and he abstains from any participation in making the agreement.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does the Act prohibit _Councilmember Drew Combs, who is employed by Facebook, from 
taking part in property-related applications filed with the City by Facebook, by an owner of 
property leased to Facebook, or by property owners in close proximity to Facebook? 
2. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Combs from taking part in amendments to the 
City's General Plan or the Transportation Master Plan?2 Because your questions regarding the provisions of the Act are general in nature, we are providing only 
informal assistance in response to your questions under the Act. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with 
the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).)  
3. Assuming Councilmember Combs has a financial interest in a development agreement 
between the City and Facebook, would the remote interest in Section 1091(b)(2) apply to allow the 
City to nonetheless enter into or amend an agreement with Facebook?

CONCLUSION: 1. The Act prohibits Councilmember Combs from taking part in property-related 
applications filed by Facebook or by an owner of property leased to Facebook. Moreover, the Act 
would prohibit him from taking part in property-related applications by owners of property in close 
proximity if a prudent person would-find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision would 
contribute to a change in Facebook's stock price. 
2. The Act prohibits Councilmember Combs from taking part in decisions such as the 
General Plan amendments or Transportation Master Plan if Facebook is explicitly involved in the 
decision. Accordingly, Councilmember Combs is prohibited from taking part in decisions initiated 
by Facebook. In addition, the Act would prohibit him from taking part in amendments to the 
General Plan or the Transportation Master Plan if the Face book is the subject of the action or the 
action is directed solely at Facebook. Accordingly, Councilmember Combs may be prohibited from 
taking part in the decisions if they are limited or directed towards Facebook or its property. For 
other decisions not initiated by Facebook and not otherwise limited or directed towards Facebook or 
its property, Councilmember Combs is prohibited from taking part in the decisions only if a prudent 
person would find it reasonably foreseeable that the decision would contribute to a change in 
Facebook's stock price. 
3. The exception under Section 1091(b)(2) would apply to allow the City to enter into or 
amend an agreement with Facebook so long as Councilmember Combs discloses his financial 
interest in the agreement, that disclosure is noted in the official records, and he abstains from any 
participation in making the agreement. 
·

(Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a written response from either entity. (Section 1097 .1 ( c )( 4 ). ) We are also required to advise you that, for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(S).) QUESTIONS 1. Does the Act prohibit _Councilmember Drew Combs, who is employed by Facebook, from taking part in property-related applications filed with the City by Facebook, by an owner of property leased to Facebook, or by property owners in close proximity to Facebook? 2. Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Combs from taking part in amendments to the City's General Plan or the Transportation Master Plan? 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 2 Because your questions regarding the provisions of the Act are general in nature, we are providing only informal assistance in response to your questions under the Act. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) File No. A-18-273 Page No. 2 3. Assuming Councilmember Combs has a financial interest in a development agreement between the City and Facebook, would the remote interest in Section 1091(b)(2) apply to allow the City to nonetheless enter into or amend an agreement with Facebook?
